ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oolong tea beverage class; no specific entry for honey-sweetened oolong tea. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers oolong tea as a category; no specific code for honey-sweetened oolong tea drink. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227493005 covers oolong tea as a substance; no specific concept for honey-sweetened oolong tea beverage. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup of wildflower honey oolong tea, approximately 500 ml as served at KOI Thé Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, oolong, brewed, prepared with tap water; and Sugars, honey (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed oolong tea (FDC ID 171917) with USDA SR Legacy data for honey (FDC ID 169640), proportioned to reflect a typical large-cup honey oolong tea beverage (approximately 500 ml water with 15 g honey per 100 ml serving basis). Per-100g values represent the ready-to-drink beverage. Oolong tea base contributes negligible macronutrients; honey contributes carbohydrates and sugars. Estimation is conservative and consistent with published beverage composition data.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value from International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 (Atkinson et al.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for wildflower honey oolong tea as a beverage. GI estimated using honey as the primary glycemic contributor. Honey has a published GI of approximately 55 per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The oolong tea base contributes no glycemic carbohydrates. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 16.5 g) / 100 = 9.1. Serving carbohydrate = 3.3 g per 100 ml × 500 ml = 16.5 g. GI of 55 is classified as low per the standard boundary used by the University of Sydney GI Database (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70).

hypertension.guidance: With approximately 1.5 mg sodium per 100 ml, wildflower honey oolong tea is a low-sodium beverage and is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though total daily sugar intake should still be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added honey contributes free sugars and moderate glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The wildflower honey in this beverage contributes approximately 16.5 g of free sugars per large cup (500 ml) with a glycemic load of approximately 9.1; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should account for this sugar load within their daily carbohydrate budget and consider requesting reduced honey or unsweetened oolong tea.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and sodium; oolong tea polyphenols may be cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wildflower honey oolong tea contains negligible saturated fat and sodium; oolong tea polyphenols have been associated with modest cardioprotective effects in observational studies, though the added honey means this beverage should be consumed in moderation as part of a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wildflower Honey Oolong Tea (Large) is a sweetened tea beverage served at KOI Thé, a Taiwanese bubble tea chain with outlets in Singapore. The drink is prepared by brewing oolong tea leaves and sweetening the infusion with wildflower honey, resulting in a lightly floral, mildly sweet beverage with the characteristic roasted and floral notes of oolong tea complemented by the complex, aromatic sweetness of wildflower honey. The large size (大杯) is approximately 500 ml. Oolong tea is a partially oxidised Chinese tea that sits between green and black tea in terms of oxidation level, offering a nuanced flavour profile ranging from floral and fruity to toasty and woody depending on the cultivar and roasting degree. Wildflower honey, derived from the nectar of multiple wildflower species, contributes natural sugars (primarily fructose and glucose) and trace minerals. Nutritionally, the beverage is low in calories (approximately 63 kcal per large cup), contains negligible protein and fat, and derives its carbohydrate content almost entirely from the honey (approximately 16.5 g sugars per serving). The glycemic load per large cup is approximately 9.1, and the glycemic index is 55 (low per University of Sydney GI Database boundary of ≤55). Oolong tea polyphenols, including theaflavins and catechins, may offer antioxidant and modest cardioprotective benefits. The beverage is dairy-free, gluten-free, and suitable for vegetarians but not strict vegans due to the honey content. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor the free sugar contribution from honey, while the beverage is generally well-tolerated for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its very low sodium and saturated fat content.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional analysis of KOI Thé wildflower honey oolong tea exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed oolong tea and honey in proportions consistent with a typical honey tea beverage. GI is estimated from published honey GI values. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach; exact honey quantity per serving at KOI Thé is not publicly disclosed in an approved source.
notes: Fat_g is naturally zero as oolong tea and dilute honey solution contain no fat by nature. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for brewed oolong tea and honey. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero in a liquid tea beverage. Protein_g is trace (0.03 g per 100 ml) derived from honey; effectively negligible. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for dilute brewed oolong tea with honey. Vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in USDA SR Legacy data for this beverage type. Vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in USDA SR Legacy data for this beverage type. Vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in USDA SR Legacy data for this beverage type. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in USDA SR Legacy data for this beverage type. Vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not present in plant-based tea or honey per USDA SR Legacy. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is below detection threshold in USDA SR Legacy data for this beverage type. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in USDA SR Legacy data for this beverage type. Zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in USDA SR Legacy data for this beverage type. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage type. Glycemic_index is 55, classified as low per the University of Sydney GI Database boundary (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70); gi_category corrected to 'low' in this iteration. GI is based on honey as the primary glycemic contributor per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values. GL per large serving (500 ml) = 9.1. The food_name uses 'Large' to reflect the 大 (large cup) designation in the original Chinese description. Cuisine is Taiwanese as KOI Thé is a Taiwanese tea brand. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish multilingual requirements, as this beverage is served in Singapore.
